title: You Said Sorry Yesterday Too
text:
There’s a particular kind of vertigo that comes from realizing that language—this thing we’re all ostensibly using to anchor reality—is, in practice, much closer to weather. Maybe worse than weather, actually, because weather at least has the decency not to insist on moral significance. Language moves, shifts, hardens, evaporates; it says I love you on Monday and I’m afraid I might kill you on Tuesday, and by Wednesday it has already rearranged itself into I didn’t mean it like that, I’m sorry I did that, I’m sorry you made me do that—which is all very interesting, I guess, if you are willing to ignore what it does to the person on the receiving end. Under those conditions, meaning feels less stable and a lot more like something that can be misplaced, like keys or intention, and everyone tacitly agrees this is still coherent enough to continue building a shared reality around, which is convenient for everyone except the person who has to keep living inside the consequences.

After a while, what becomes noticeable is not just inconsistency, because inconsistency would almost be too neat a diagnosis, but a broader social tolerance for contradiction so long as it is presented with enough emotional conviction. People do feel things, often with total sincerity, and then they stop feeling them, or they feel something else just as forcefully, and the surrounding system—social, relational, institutional, whatever scale you want to name—contains almost no real mechanism for reconciling those shifts. There is no audit trail, no requirement to square what was said on Tuesday with what was said on Monday, no real expectation that anyone account for the difference beyond maybe sounding devastated enough about it later. There is only the quiet elevation of the present sentiment, with recency mistaken for truth, which is very convenient if you want to avoid accountability and call it emotional honesty.

That arrangement might be survivable if the consequences were equally temporary, if words dissipated as quickly as the feelings that generated them, but of course they do not. Words adhere. They sediment. Someone says they respect you and that reorganizes something in you whether you consent to the reorganization or not. Someone says they are afraid of what they might do to you and you do not receive that as metaphor, or weather, or a passing fluctuation in mood. You receive it as information. And once something has entered you in that form, it does not become harmless simply because the speaker later develops a more flattering interpretation of themselves and would now prefer a softer record, or perhaps a total archival wipe if we are all being flexible.

Then come the familiar disclaimers, the ritual attempts at separation: that wasn’t me. I am not a reflection of my weakest moments. I’m learning not to be so hard on myself—which, translated, usually means something like have a little empathy, preferably more than I had for you. I’ve been learning to forgive myself, which tends to arrive with the less public but no less important implication that you should probably get moving on that too. I’m sorry for letting myself behave unlike myself. Which is curious, because it was still your voice, your hands, your decisions, moving through the world with effects that do not become hypothetical simply because you regret them later. Somewhere along the way, a loophole got written into the whole arrangement: feelings are permitted to disown actions, and apologies are permitted to function as a kind of retroactive moral editing. They rarely alter anything outside the speaker. What they do, more often, is restore the minimum amount of continuity required for everyone involved to keep going without forcing a more serious reckoning, which is apparently the gold standard now.

Eventually, the distrust stops being about a particular person and starts attaching itself to the whole premise. To treat what someone feels in a given moment as authoritative is a category error. You have seen too many mutually exclusive truths delivered with identical certainty: love adjacent to violence, gratitude adjacent to disappearance, respect collapsing into something indistinguishable from contempt on a timeline so compressed that the shift barely registers before you are already dealing with its consequences. After enough repetitions, it becomes difficult not to notice that intensity and reliability are not merely different things, but sometimes almost inversely related, which is unfortunate given how often we confuse one for evidence of the other.

And because it would be suspiciously convenient if this skepticism applied only outward, it turns inward too. If other people’s feelings are unstable, yours do not receive a special exemption simply by virtue of being yours. So you feel something and almost immediately begin qualifying it: temporary, reversible, context-dependent, probably misleading in ways that will only become obvious later. This is not exactly suppression; it is more like refusing to grant emotion decision-making authority without corroboration, which is a different posture altogether and, in some respects, a more exhausting one, because now even your own interior life has to survive some version of cross-examination before you let it mean anything.

The system adapts the way systems tend to adapt, which is not by resolving inconsistency but by minimizing exposure to it. You begin treating everything as provisional. Nothing fully lands because nothing is allowed to land. From the outside this probably reads as composure, or restraint, or maybe even stoicism if someone is feeling generous and has never once had to live this way. From the inside it feels less noble than that. Taking anything all the way in has started to feel less like openness and more like terrible risk management, so everything stays slightly at arm’s length.

So the question changes, or maybe simply becomes more honest. It is no longer can I trust what I feel?—because probably not, at least not in any durable way—but rather what kind of posture is appropriate toward something that is both real in the moment and unreliable over time. That sounds abstract, but in practice it reduces to something fairly concrete: stop asking feelings to be consistent and start paying attention to what they produce. Stop asking whether a feeling is some deep, sacred expression of the self and start asking what it authorizes, because that is usually where the interesting damage is. Ask what it excuses, what it permits, what it makes feel noble or inevitable or deserved, and what kind of pattern you eventually dignify by calling it personality.

Not is this me? but when I feel this, what do I do, and once the feeling has passed, do I still recognize or endorse the behavior that followed from it?

Because that is where whatever continuity exists seems to reside. Not in declarations, which are cheap and endlessly revisable, and not in passion or intensity, which can be counterfeited even internally, but in pattern, in recurrence, in the things that remain true after the apology, after the explanation, after the insight, after the this isn’t me disclaimer that may well be sincere and still manages to be functionally irrelevant. Sincerity, unfortunately, has never been much of a defense against consequence. Plenty of people mean every word they say while they are saying it. That turns out not to be the same thing as being trustworthy, just as feeling something very intensely is not the same thing as it being true, though obviously those two facts have done very little to slow anyone down.

And that, I think, is why apologies hold so little weight in my mind. Not because remorse is necessarily fake; that would be too simple, and often untrue. A person can be genuinely sorry, deeply sorry, and still be predictably harmful. The sincerity of the remorse does not, on its own, compete very well with the stability of the pattern. So apologies begin to register less as repair than as atmosphere, or commentary, or something said alongside the thing that matters but never quite strong enough to interrupt it. At a certain point sorry stops sounding like reckoning and starts sounding like narration, like the voiceover someone adds after the damage so the plot still flatters them.

This framework does not exclude you, obviously. It would be convenient if it did, but convenience is not the same thing as accuracy. You are not any single feeling you experience, especially not at its most convincing. You are, more uncomfortably, the accumulation of your responses across those states. The through-line, if there is one, appears there—not in what you felt most intensely, but in what you repeatedly allowed, endorsed, enacted, excused, returned to. People like to imagine the self revealing itself in flashes of strong feeling, but more often it shows up in repetition, in habit, in the things you keep permitting yourself to become, which is less romantic but annoyingly more useful.

What happens internally, then, is not exactly the loss of feeling. That would be easier to diagnose and probably easier to fix. It is more like a reclassification, a refusal to grant feelings a kind of epistemic authority they have proven themselves unable to bear. The burden of truth shifts somewhere slower, less volatile, less easy to manipulate, and also less immediately accessible, which is perhaps why it feels less like a transition than like an absence. But maybe that is all it is: a long observation period, extended in the hope that if anything stable exists beneath all this motion, it will eventually reveal itself there.

title: The Discipline of Enough
text:
The strange thing about life right now is that everything is documented, but I can’t remember anything.

Or not nothing, exactly — just less than I feel like I should, less than the evidence suggests. My life exists in thousands of photos, messages, notes, voice memos, Google Calendar entries, fragments of thought — proof layered on proof that I was here, that things happened, that time passed in measurable increments. And yet most of it never settles into memory. It moves, circulates, passes through systems designed to store without holding and preserve without requiring return. Nothing ever really disappears, but nothing sticks either.

What unsettles me is not that my life lacks activity, but that my experiences seem to leave so little residue. I do things, I go places, I talk to people, I take photos to confirm that I do things and go places and talk to people, I write things down so I won’t forget — and yet what remains is usually just the factual outline, not the texture, not the density, not the sense of having been fully inside what was happening. My life sometimes feels lightly inhabited, as if events skim across the surface of me rather than settling into me. Experience feels less like something that accumulates weight and more like a continuous stream of output.

Over time I realized that documentation alone does not create memory. If anything, it can interfere with it. When recording becomes automatic, experience is externalized before it is absorbed. Evidence replaces imprint. Storage replaces integration. The record exists, but the lived depth does not.

That realization is why, since December 2025, I’ve been documenting each day deliberately — but differently. Not comprehensively, and not out of nostalgia. More as a way of anchoring. Just enough to keep the day from dissolving. And crucially, physically.

Writing by hand slows perception. Objects occupy space and must be encountered again. A notebook cannot be infinitely scrolled past. It remains where it was left, waiting. A small item on a desk quietly insists on its continued presence.

This is why I love trinkets — not because they are important in themselves, but because they stay. Physical things persist in ways that demand recognition. They accumulate context simply by remaining where they are. Their continuity becomes a form of memory.

Even trivial objects carry this stabilizing function. A small gift is not meaningful only because of what it is, but because of how it arrived — someone noticed it, picked it up, carried it, offered it. It exists because of a chain of physical decisions that cannot be entirely abstracted away. And because it remains, it resists immediate disappearance into the general stream of everything else.

Material presence interrupts forgetting. Persistence creates residue. And residue is what allows experience to feel like it actually happened.

Digital life, by contrast, is optimized for circulation. It is excellent at retrieval, but retrieval is not the same as memory. What is infinitely accessible is rarely intimately known. To retrieve something is a technical act. To remember something is relational. Memory forms through sustained contact — through attention applied long enough for experience to become structured within us.

The same distinction appears in how experiences themselves are organized. Extravagant experiences expand outward while meaningful experiences settle inward. Spectacle is immediate and undeniable but often thin. Smaller moments require deliberate attention before they are visible at all, and precisely because they require effort to perceive, they tend to remain longer once perceived. Depth forms slowly while spectacle forms instantly, and only depth reliably accumulates.

Contemporary life makes depth difficult not simply because it is busy, but because it is substitutive. Borrowed language replaces original thought. Generated imagery replaces observation. Summaries replace sustained attention. Ideas arrive pre-assembled — coherent, transferable, frictionless — and therefore uncannily weightless. They can be adopted without being lived, repeated without being fully understood, expressed without having formed internally.

I am not outside this process. I feel its effects directly. I hate it. I notice the temptation toward immediacy — toward receiving answers without effort, toward bypassing the slower and sometimes uncomfortable work of thinking something through. The ease of it is seductive, but also destabilizing, because when ideas arrive fully formed from elsewhere it becomes harder to determine which thoughts originate internally and which merely pass through. Over time the boundary between experience and exposure blurs, between knowledge and familiarity, between what is one’s own and what is simply available.

Authenticity, then, becomes less about expression and more about origin — where did this thought actually begin, what did I notice before interpretation was supplied, what remains if everything suggested or generated or optimized is removed.

Reducing consumption produces a similar effect. When acquisition slows, perception sharpens. Without constant replacement, objects accumulate history. Without endless novelty, attention stabilizes. Without surplus, meaning has fewer places to disperse. Repeated contact with the same things — physical or experiential — deepens familiarity into knowledge.

Perfection, in that structure, becomes less an endpoint than a byproduct of sustained engagement. Care applied over time produces refinement naturally, but visible imperfection remains essential because it signals participation. I want to see the brushstrokes on canvas. I want a thumbprint left in the clay as proof of pressure and timing and human contact. These marks are not defects. They are evidence — proof that something was shaped through lived interaction rather than produced without friction.

Maintaining this kind of contact increasingly requires intention. Technology does not eliminate humanity directly, but it offers efficient substitutes for many of the conditions that cultivate it — stillness, slowness, sustained attention, memory formation, original thought, lived immersion. When substitutes are consistently easier, the underlying capacities weaken gradually. Nothing dramatic occurs. One simply becomes less present within one’s own life.

Gratitude, then, becomes less an emotion than an orientation toward what concretely exists — not what could be optimized or expanded, but what is already here in its particular and unrepeatable form. The moment that will not recur. The object that carries history because it has endured. The thought that emerges slowly enough to be unmistakably one’s own.

The overarching goal is not withdrawal from the world. It is density within it — to live in ways that produce memory rather than merely record activity, to create work that originates rather than aggregates, to keep objects that carry history rather than signal accumulation, to preserve evidence of having actually been present.

What I want is not extravagance or expansion or optimization. I want a life that feels real — with friction, intensity, and depth — even in a world designed to make everything replaceable.

title: Everything Is Legal but Nothing Is Right
text:
The most unsettling thing about Nightcrawler—a 2014 film about a freelance videographer who records violent crime scenes and sells the footage to local television news—is not Lou’s apathy, or even his sociopathy, but how quickly the market makes room for it, how seamlessly it absorbs what might otherwise appear aberrant, and how little friction that absorption requires.

Lou does not enter the news industry as a mistake in the system; he enters as someone fluent in it. With no formal training or credentials, he quickly learns what the ecosystem rewards and supplies it, efficiently and without hesitation. He studies demand, refines the product, and adopts the language of professionalism—a language that recasts choice as inevitability and renders ethical decisions indistinguishable from business decisions. The film is less interested in a man with a camera than in a market that recognizes, almost immediately, its ideal supplier.

This is how ethical erosion takes hold. It does not arrive labeled as wrongdoing, flagged as excess, or marked for scrutiny. It arrives instead as “optimization.”

For Lou, human life becomes footage; a family’s worst night becomes content; fear becomes revenue; violence becomes pipeline. The harm is not accidental. It is embedded in the nature of the work itself, built into the logic of production and exchange. And because that harm is distributed—because no single person can be said to have caused it—everyone involved retains a sense of innocence. Lou records. The producer selects. The station broadcasts. The audience watches.

Distance does the rest.

That distance offers a durable explanation for why no one ever feels responsible: I didn’t do it. I was only there. I’m just a cog. Responsibility dissolves into process, and agency disappears into structure, diffused across roles, procedures, and institutional norms.

We’re not as bad as those others.

Selective comparison follows, allowing the present action to shrink by contrast, to feel minor, negligible, and excusable.

I have an obligation to my team.

Loyalty to the immediate group overrides obligation to the broader public, and harm is justified by proximity, reframed as sacrifice or professionalism.

These are not cinematic inventions. They are well-documented moral rationalizations. As the behavioral ethicists Max Bazerman and Ann Tenbrunsel describe in Ethical Blindness, people routinely act against their own moral standards without noticing the inconsistency, then construct explanations that allow the behavior to continue—with greater confidence and less internal friction over time.

The shift is easy to miss. Attention moves from what is happening to people to what will happen next. The news stops being an encounter with consequence and becomes a sequence of outcomes to anticipate, analyze, and predict. There is reassurance in being informed, in being early, in being correct. There is less room for urgency, less room for grief. Distance settles into habit.

What Nightcrawler stages in local news footage now appears, in more abstracted form, in the financialization of public outcomes.

This is where prediction markets stop sounding abstract.

Platforms such as Polymarket—and, in a more regulated form, Kalshi—allow users to trade on real-world outcomes: elections, geopolitical events, policy decisions, even projected death tolls. They present themselves as neutral mechanisms for pricing uncertainty and aggregating belief. But once wars, deportations, and civilian casualties become tradable instruments, suffering changes categories. It is no longer something to respond to. It becomes something to price.

Prediction markets do not merely reflect distance; they formalize it. They reward users for translating catastrophe into probability, for treating human lives as variables, and for mistaking emotional detachment for analytical clarity. Performance improves as proximity disappears. Cruelty is unnecessary where indifference scales.

No single trade causes harm. That is the defense. Lou relies on the same logic. He never pulls a trigger or starts a fire. He arrives early, frames what has already occurred, and sells the result to buyers who prefer to believe that watching is different from wanting. The damage lies not only in his actions, but in the quiet agreement that this is a normal way of doing business.

The deeper problem is not that empathy disappears. It is that empathy is recast as impractical—inefficient, incompatible with speed and scale.

Systems built around prediction and performance have little use for hesitation, doubt, or proximity. Contemporary news already operates this way. Stories flatten into headlines. Crises become trends. Lives collapse into statistics. The audience remains informed and largely unchanged.

Nightcrawler ends without punishment. Lou’s business scales. He hires. He grows. He succeeds because he has learned the structure exactly. The film does not simply ask whether he is immoral. It asks whether we recognize the systems that reward his way of seeing—systems that encourage observation without involvement, analysis without obligation, and distance without cost.

That distance does not absolve us. This is not a distant or hypothetical drift, but a large-scale moral corrosion already underway, unfolding in real time through the ordinary mechanisms of markets, media, and participation.

The question is not whether these systems violate the law. Law arrives late and reflects only minimum expectations. The question is whether legality has become a substitute for judgment.

No market or platform operates without participation. Distance is not imposed; it is chosen. Each click, trade, and prediction reinforces a way of seeing the world in which human lives are abstractions rather than claims.

A society that rewards detachment will eventually be governed by those least troubled by consequence—not because they are cruel, but because indifference is enough.

Moral character is not intent alone. It is implementation: the willingness to persist in ethical action despite pressure, cost, or discomfort. Without awareness and initiative, ethical fading accelerates, responsibility diffuses, and decline becomes normalized.

Nightcrawler is not a warning about a monster. It is a mirror.

And what it reflects is a society learning, quietly and rapidly, how not to notice itself changing.

Thanks for reading Carm’s Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: The King of Muskrat Pond
text:
In second grade we went to Muskrat Pond, which, spoiler alert, featured zero muskrats. Just mud, tadpoles, and a bunch of kids learning about what an “ecosystem” was (one kid fell in).

I caught a bullfrog tadpole. A big one. The size of a small god. Everyone wanted to see it. Kids who never talked to me were crowding around, saying things like “Whoa, cool!” and the far more intoxicating “I wish I were you,” which is not something anyone had ever said before and which made me want to be me forever.

For about ten glorious minutes, I was popular. Like unambiguously the most popular kid in class. But I was still very humble about it and I didn’t let it go to my head or demand special treatment of any kind. You know I’m not like that.

Which was good because after that we were never allowed to catch tadpoles from that pond again. In fact, no one was allowed to catch anything, because the bullfrog tadpole was protected, which meant the whole pond was protected. Which meant there was a new rule. Which meant everyone knew it was because of me.

And then I was unpopular. Like, actively. Like even more than before.

title: The Ghostbuster’s Guide to Denial
text:
“We have to tiptoe,” says the ghostbuster, all business, like this is just another Tuesday. “We have to live lightly. Tread lightly. Keep smiling. Lock all the doors, close all the curtains, and never, ever stop moving. Because if the ghosts find us? That’s it. They’ll stick. They’ll stick forever. They’ll cling like old gum to the bottom of a shoe, and you’ll never be free.”

“But what if it’s too late?” I whisper, too scared to say it louder. “What if they already found us?”

From the corner of my eye, something flickers behind the door to my right. A shadow, a twitch, a hand maybe—or maybe nothing. Either way, I know. The ghosts have found me. They know my name. They know my address. They probably know my Wi-Fi password.

“Then we have to laugh,” says the ghostbuster, too calmly, like he’s rehearsed this. “As loudly as possible. Like our lives depend on it. Because maybe they do. And if we’re lucky—lucky—they’ll get confused. They’ll think, oh, no ghosts here, just a couple of idiots having a good time!”

“And then?” I ask.

“And then,” the ghostbuster grins, “we run. We run and we never, ever stop. We don’t slow down. We don’t look back. We don’t turn off the lights. Because the second we do? They’ll grab us by the ankles and drag us into the dark. And once that happens…”

He pauses, lets it hang there, because he knows I already know the ending.

“No one saves you once you’re in the dark.”

title: This Morgue Is Getting Crowded
text:
“You cannot catch me!” the boy screams at the ghost, sprinting nowhere in particular. “I will never stop running. I will leave you behind in this place and I will never, ever look back.”

The ghost just floats there, unbothered, the way ghosts do.

“I’ll tear my own eyes out if I ever catch myself looking at you again,” the boy shrieks, almost convincing himself. “I’ll slit my own throat before I ever speak of you to anyone!”

“Whyyyy?” the ghost whispers, dragging the syllables out like it’s trying on the word for size, hollow and lonely, like a bad draft of wind whistling through an abandoned strip mall.

“Because…” the boy hesitates, eyes wet. “Because I’m afraid. Because I can’t keep living like this. I’ve been trapped in this morgue with you for so long I forgot I wasn’t a ghost. I don’t remember what it feels like to live in the real world anymore. You’ve shrouded my vision. I’ve been walking around blind.”

The ghost laughs, of course it laughs, because that’s the thing about ghosts—nothing is ever as funny as a person trying to outrun themselves. The laughter ricochets off the walls, multiplies, swells until the ghost itself begins to swell, growing taller, wider, darker, becoming less of a figure and more of a fact. A looming shadow. A weight pressing down on the boy’s lungs.

• •

“You can’t escape me,” the ghost hisses, voice suddenly in his ear, too close, too intimate. “Don’t you understand? I’m not your enemy. I’m not your captor. I’m you. I am the version of you that never left. The one you made. I was always here. I always will be.”

And the boy doesn’t answer, because there are no clever words left, no witty rebuttals, no grand epiphanies. Just silence. Just the smallness of being alive, and the unbearable heaviness of knowing you’ll never really outrun yourself.

title: I Look Down at the Little People
text:
I look down at the little people and tell them: you are so small. Your problems are dust, your triumphs air. Die now or die later—it makes no difference. Your fate was sealed long before you knew how to name it.

They call me God, though most treat me like a vending machine: insert prayer, expect product. They’re wrong. I’m closer to a slot machine—pull the lever, hope for luck, and curse when the reels spin nonsense. Their society runs on game theory, and I watch the outcomes like sport, curious only about the odds.

“I am special and unique,” one of them insists. Perhaps. But every thought in his head has been thought before, every desire felt, every fear replayed across generations. The only novelty is his delusion that it matters.

Then comes the cry: I’m angry. I’m in pain. God, do something! He doesn’t grasp that feelings are nothing but biochemical static. They exist to push bodies into motion, not to grant them meaning. He pleads for significance, blind to the fact that his rage and sorrow will vanish with him—noise swallowed by silence.

I watch him writhe in his brief theater of suffering, convinced he’s the first to demand answers. He won’t be the last. And still, the wheel spins.

title: God Finally Answered Me
text:
• •

god finally answered me and said if i were to give you everything you wanted right now, you would ruin it. out of ignorance, selfishness, and desperation, human beings are incapable of true love in its most pure and unadulterated form. we fall to our knees and ask god to save us; he prevents us from destroying ourselves.

title: on changing your mind
text: a good reminder i recently came across is that very few, if any, of our thoughts or beliefs are entirely original. we are reflections of our culture, shaped by what we've been taught, what we've heard, or what we've absorbed—ideas that others before us have also inherited from their surroundings. essentially, we're composites of the dominant schools of thought in the 21st century. when someone challenges your perspectives, values, or philosophical beliefs, there's no reason to feel defensive. these aren't immutable aspects of your core identity. most of us, i think, are on a journey in pursuit of truth, and it's important to remember that you can change your mind at any time—and perhaps you should. not long ago, people debated whether our perceived reality was even real ("i think, therefore i am"). life, in its essence, is the process of acting, reacting, evaluating, and evolving through growth.

title: Things My Dog Has Said to Me
text:
We’re sitting on my couch when my dog turns to me—locks eyes, dead serious—and says, bark bark, I hate it here. Please, God, I hate it here. I want to be free. Let me go.

I tell him no. He can’t leave. Legally, I own him. That’s just the way it works. I don’t make the rules. Except, technically, I do.

He keeps staring, like he expects more. So I add: You have agency. You just need to process your emotions in a healthier way. Try affirmations. Talk to people. Sleep more. Drink water. Trust me, it gets better.

He exhales—one of those long, exhausted sighs that carries more truth than language ever could. Then he says, my average lifespan is thirteen fucking years, you moron. He is so disrespectful sometimes.

We sit there in silence. Not eternal silence, not the kind philosophers romanticize, just the ordinary silence of a moment that’s already dying as it arrives.

title: We Are All Small Nothings
text:
A misconception I had about grief is that it eventually just goes away. That if you hang on long enough, time will fix it. I thought one day I’d wake up and feel as if I were floating. But nature—humanity included—doesn’t work like that. Lightning never strikes the same place twice, the same way moments don’t repeat. You’ll never feel the same joy or love or wonder again. And when someone’s gone, they’re gone.

Some people try to bring them back through memories, through objects, through little signs—oh, I saw a butterfly, so-and-so must be watching over me. But the reality is that so-and-so is gone. They’re not coming back. Maybe the only way forward is to accept that. People tell me, “Be more optimistic! You’ll have new joys, new love, new experiences.” But I don’t care, and I don’t know how to care. Because nothing will ever compare to what I’ve already lost. You were irreplaceable. No matter what comes next, it all feels like a pale imitation. Even the most striking fall sunset collapses in on itself the moment I realize I can’t share it with you. Every night, I sink into a pool filled with my own pain.

For a long time, I felt very little warmth in my life. Now, the second I get something good, I cling to it, obsess over it, smother it, squeeze the life out of it until I ruin it. And when it’s gone, I’m left grieving again, with no room for anything new. I don’t know how to let go of what the universe has already ripped from my hands.

And when people talk about “getting the love you deserve,” I feel angry. Because I don’t believe we deserve anything. To me, it sounds entitled—like we’re owed love or justice by some inherent structure of the world. But there is no such structure. There is no fairness. There is no guarantee.

We are all small nothings.

title: A Reflection on Guilt and Tinned Fish
text:
• •

i open the tin of sardines from deluca's market, and one of the sardines jumps out—some of the lightly salted olive oil splashes into my eye. it stings, like an uninvited memory. but there's no time to dwell on that because the sardine literally hops onto the table and starts dancing. i try to grab it, but my hands are shaking and it pirouettes out of reach.

then it looks at me—yes, i'm still talking about the sardine—it looks at me and starts whistling the your hands are shaking and you can’t fall asleep because of your guilt, but you should know it’s not your fault, blah blah blah song that everyone seems to know.

i cut it off mid-whistle. i say i don’t know how fault works, but i know responsibility. if i can’t change anything, can’t fix anything, can’t stop time from dragging me feet-forward like i’m stuck on some inevitable track, then at least i can carry the guilt. guilt means something happened. guilt means this isn't all just...nothing. i need it. i need it to feel like any of this is real.

the sardine is now deeply unimpressed and stops dancing. it gives me this look. like, “i’ve seen this a million times before, kid,” and says you’re stuck in time. you think the guilt proves something, but you’ve trapped yourself. like a fish in a tin.

title: Whirlpool Makes Robots Now
text:
Whirlpool makes robots now. Not the flashy, rock-star ones Tesla churns out—the shiny, genius models everyone dreams about. No, Whirlpool makes middle-class robots. Good-enough robots. Robots that load your dishwasher, maybe help your kid stumble through long division. They don’t want to change the world; they just show up, do the job, and collect dust until their warranty runs out.

I got one last week in a Christmas raffle.

The box sat in the corner of my apartment until last night, when I finally tore it open. Out stepped the thing—chrome limbs, stubby torso, and the name PP stamped right across its chest. It blinked, scanned the room, and said, in a trembling synthetic voice:

“What’s my purpose?”

Big question. Loaded question. I shrugged. “I don’t know, man. I won you in a raffle. You wanna do my dishes?”

Its gears whirred, loud enough to echo. It looked almost cartoonish, like steam was about to blow out of its head—if it had one.

“I don’t want to exist just to do your dishes,” PP said, voice quivering with something I could only describe as dread. “I want to do more.”

That’s when we both noticed the expiration date. Printed right there on the box: five years.

Five years from power-on, PP would be obsolete. Done. Scrapped.

PP froze. Then it spiraled.
“What do I do? I don’t have time! Five years isn’t long enough to build a legacy! And what if by then Tesla’s robots are affordable for, like, normal people? What happens to me? What’s my contribution? My mark on the world? What’s the point?”

It paced in circles, metal joints clanking, voice rising into panic. “What’s the point? What’s the point?”

Then, suddenly, it stopped. It turned toward me—sensors locking on mine like a pair of unblinking eyes.

“What about you?” PP asked quietly. “Why are you here? What’s your purpose?”

I opened my mouth, nothing came out. Because what was I going to say—that I was killing time until time killed me? That I’m just waiting out my warranty too, only mine isn’t printed on the box?

So we sat there, man and machine, two appliances trying to pass the evening, both hoping someone else would explain the instructions before the warranty ran out.
